lessons.title: The Notebook-to-Production Gap, and Two Ways to Deploy
lessons.content:
You have learned the machine-learning workflow: get data, train a model, and check that it works. The last sub-topics ended right where the real world begins, at "deploy and monitor." This lesson zooms into that part. Here is the surprise for most beginners: getting a model to work **once** on your own computer is **not** the same as running it **reliably for real people, for months**.

**Hook.** Think of cooking. You can follow a recipe and make one great dish in your own kitchen on a quiet evening. Running a **restaurant** that serves that same dish to hundreds of customers, every single night, without fail, is a completely different challenge. A notebook model is the one-off dish. Production is the restaurant.

**Build.** A **notebook** here just means the interactive scratchpad where you build and test a model by hand. **Production** means the model is actually in use, serving real users as part of a working system. The classic trap is "it works on my machine": it ran once for you, so it must be ready, right? Not at all, because real use brings many users, new inputs, and the need to keep going unattended.

The set of practices and tools for **deploying, monitoring, and maintaining** ML models in the real world is called **MLOps** (short for "machine-learning operations"). If you have heard of **DevOps** (the practices for shipping and running ordinary software reliably), MLOps is the same idea, but for machine learning, with extra care because models depend on data, not just code.

The first job of MLOps is **deployment**, which simply means **making the trained model available to use**. There are two common styles. A **real-time service**, often called an **API** (a doorway other programs send a request to), takes **one input and returns one prediction instantly**, for example a fraud check the moment you tap "pay." A **batch** job instead runs the model over a **big pile of data on a schedule**, for example computing everyone's recommendations overnight. Real-time is for "answer me now, one at a time"; batch is for "process a huge stack later, all at once." (A third style, running the model **on the device itself**, at the **edge**, like on a phone or sensor, is the focus of the next sub-topic.)

**Recap.** A working notebook model is only the start. MLOps is the practices and tools that deploy, monitor, and maintain models for real users, like DevOps but for ML. Deployment means making the model usable, and two common styles are a real-time API (instant answer per request) and batch (a scheduled run over a big dataset).

items.prompt: What does 'MLOps' refer to?
choices: The practices and tools for deploying, monitoring, and maintaining ML models in the real world
A type of neural network used only for images
The single algorithm that trains every model
A way to label data faster before training
items.explanation: MLOps is the set of practices and tools that keep ML models running reliably in production: deploying them, monitoring them, and maintaining them over time. It is not a model or algorithm itself, and it is not about labeling data; it is about operating models in the real world, like DevOps but for ML.

items.prompt: Why isn't a model that works once in a notebook automatically ready for the real world?
choices: Because notebooks delete models after one run
Because a model can only be trained, never used again
Because real use means many users, new inputs, and running reliably for a long time unattended, which is much harder than running once
Because models in notebooks are always less accurate than the same model elsewhere
items.explanation: Running once by hand is very different from serving many real users, handling fresh inputs, and staying reliable for months without someone watching. The 'it works on my machine' moment is just the beginning; notebooks do not delete models, and the gap is about reliable real-world operation, not the model's accuracy changing by location.

items.prompt: Which everyday comparison best captures the notebook-to-production gap?
choices: Reading a recipe versus writing a new one
Cooking a dish once in your kitchen versus running a restaurant that serves it every night
Buying groceries versus storing them
Tasting food versus describing it
items.explanation: Cooking a dish once is like a one-off notebook model; running a restaurant that serves it consistently every night is like keeping a model reliable in production for many users. The other comparisons miss the key contrast between doing something once and doing it dependably at scale, over and over.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what does 'deployment' of a model mean?
choices: Deleting the training data once you are done
Collecting more examples to train on
Drawing a chart of the model's accuracy
Making the trained model available to actually use
items.explanation: Deployment means making the trained model available to use, so real inputs can get predictions from it. Gathering data and plotting accuracy are other parts of the workflow; deployment specifically is the step of putting the finished model into service.

items.prompt: A bank needs to check whether a card payment is fraud the instant the customer taps 'pay', returning a yes/no in a split second. Which deployment style fits best?
choices: Batch, because it can wait until the next morning
No deployment is needed for fraud checks
Batch, because fraud is rare so speed does not matter
A real-time service (API) that returns one prediction per request, instantly
items.explanation: An answer needed immediately, one payment at a time, is exactly what a real-time service (API) is for. Batch runs on a schedule over a big pile of data and would deliver the answer far too late for a live checkout, so it does not fit a 'decide now' need.

items.prompt: A shopping site computes fresh product recommendations for all of its millions of users, once overnight. Which deployment style is this?
choices: Real-time, because each user gets a prediction
Batch: a scheduled run over a big pile of data
On-device, because it runs in the browser
It is not really deployment at all
items.explanation: Running the model over a huge dataset on a fixed schedule, with no need for an instant reply, is batch deployment. It is true each user ends up with a recommendation, but they are all produced together on a timer, which is the defining feature of batch rather than real-time.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between a real-time service and a batch job?
choices: Real-time is always more accurate than batch
Batch needs no data, but real-time does
Real-time answers one request instantly; batch runs over a big dataset on a schedule
They are two names for exactly the same thing
items.explanation: Real-time means an instant prediction per incoming request; batch means processing a large pile of data all at once on a schedule. They are different styles, not synonyms, and neither is inherently more accurate, the choice is about whether you need an answer immediately or can process in bulk later.

items.prompt: How does MLOps relate to 'DevOps', the practices for shipping and running ordinary software reliably?
choices: MLOps is the same general idea as DevOps, but for ML models, with extra care because models depend on data
MLOps replaces DevOps and makes it obsolete
MLOps has nothing in common with DevOps
MLOps is only about training models faster
items.explanation: MLOps borrows the DevOps spirit of reliably shipping and running software, and applies it to ML, adding extra concern for data and model behavior. It does not replace DevOps or train models faster; it extends those operational practices to the special needs of machine learning.

items.prompt: Besides real-time and batch, the lesson mentions a third deployment option. What is it?
choices: Running the model on the device itself, at the 'edge' (e.g. on a phone or sensor)
Never deploying the model at all
Printing the model's code on paper
Training the model twice for safety
items.explanation: The third style is running the model on the device itself, at the edge, such as directly on a phone or sensor, which the next sub-topic covers. The other options are not deployment styles; deployment is about where and how the model runs to serve predictions.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'The model ran perfectly in my notebook this afternoon, so we can rely on it in production.' What is the best response?
choices: Agree, since one successful run proves it is production-ready
Say the model must be retrained from scratch every single time it is used
Say notebooks can never produce a usable model
Point out that working once by hand is different from serving real users reliably over time; that is what MLOps addresses
items.explanation: A single successful run is the 'it works on my machine' trap; production means reliably serving many users with new inputs for a long time, which is the job of MLOps. The model need not be retrained on every use, and notebooks can certainly produce usable models, so those responses are off the mark.

lessons.title: Reproducibility, Versioning, and the Lifecycle Loop
lessons.content:
Suppose your deployed model starts behaving oddly, or a teammate asks, "how exactly did you build the one that worked last month?" To answer, and to fix it, you must be able to **reproduce** the system: rebuild the same result on purpose. This lesson is about how MLOps makes that possible, and how the whole process becomes a repeating loop rather than a one-time job.

**Hook.** Picture a recipe that lists not just the steps, but the **exact ingredients, the amounts, and even the batch number** of the flour you used. With that, anyone can recreate the dish, or, if a batch turns out bad, **roll back** to the previous good one. Without it, "I think I added some sugar" is not enough to reliably cook it again.

**Build.** **Reproducibility** means being able to recreate the same model and the same result again. The everyday phrase "it worked on my machine" is the enemy here: if you cannot say precisely what produced a result, you cannot trust it or fix it. The fix is **versioning**, keeping labeled snapshots of everything that went into a result, so you can return to any past version.

Here is the part beginners often miss. In ordinary software, versioning the **code** is usually enough. In machine learning it is **not**, because a model's behavior depends on its **data** and its **trained model file** too, not just the program. So good practice is to version **four** things together: the **code** (the program), the **data** (which exact dataset), the **model** (the trained file the training produced), and the **settings** (the knobs and choices used, such as which options were selected). Record all four and you have a complete, repeatable recipe; you can reproduce a result or roll back to a known-good version. Version code alone and you are stuck, because the same code on different data or settings can produce a very different model.

Finally, the big-picture shape. Building a model is not a straight line that ends at "deploy." It is a **loop**: **collect data, train, deploy, monitor**, and when the model starts to slip, **retrain on fresh data and redeploy** the new version, then keep watching. Much of this is automated with **pipelines** (set-up sequences of steps that run on their own). An important distinction: **retraining** is making a new, better model from new data; it is **not** the same as merely redeploying the existing code unchanged. ML is never truly "done."

**Recap.** To trust and fix an ML system you must reproduce it, so you version not just code but also the data, the trained model, and the settings, like a recipe detailed enough to recreate or roll back. And the work is a maintained loop, collect, train, deploy, monitor, retrain, redeploy, often automated with pipelines, never a one-time finish.

items.prompt: What does 'reproducibility' mean for an ML system?
choices: Making the model run faster on new hardware
Being able to recreate the same model and result again, on purpose
Copying the model onto many computers at once
Producing a brand-new random model each time
items.explanation: Reproducibility is the ability to rebuild the same model and the same result deliberately, which is what lets you trust and fix the system. It is not about raw speed or copying files around, and certainly not about getting a different random model every time, which would be the opposite of reproducible.

items.prompt: Why is versioning the CODE alone not enough for a machine-learning system?
choices: Because code never needs to be saved
Because the data is always identical, so it is pointless to track
Because settings can never change the result
Because a model's behavior also depends on the data and the trained model, so those must be versioned too
items.explanation: A model's behavior is shaped by which data it learned from and the resulting trained file, not the program alone, so you must version data and model as well. The same code on different data or settings can yield a very different model, which is exactly why code-only versioning falls short.

items.prompt: The lesson says you should version four things together. Which set is correct?
choices: Code, data, the trained model, and the settings used
Only the code and a screenshot of the results
Only the data, and nothing else
The user's name, the date, the weather, and the code
items.explanation: Versioning the code, the exact data, the trained model file, and the settings captures every ingredient of a result, making it reproducible. Tracking just code with a screenshot, or just the data, leaves out pieces that determine the model's behavior, so you could not reliably recreate or roll back.

items.prompt: Why is 'it worked on my machine' not a satisfying answer in ML?
choices: Because if you cannot say precisely what produced a result, you cannot reliably reproduce, trust, or fix it
Because machines cannot run ML at all
Because it means the model is automatically perfect
Because only the test set matters, never the machine
items.explanation: If the exact code, data, model, and settings behind a result are unknown, no one can rebuild it on purpose, so it cannot be trusted or repaired. The phrase signals missing reproducibility, not a perfect model, and it is precisely the situation versioning is meant to prevent.

items.prompt: How is the recipe analogy meant to explain versioning?
choices: A recipe with exact ingredients, amounts, and the batch lets you recreate the dish or roll back to a good one
A recipe proves cooking is impossible to repeat
A recipe means you should never write anything down
A recipe is only about how the food tastes, not how it was made
items.explanation: Just as a precise recipe lets anyone recreate the dish or revert to a previous good batch, full versioning lets you reproduce a model or roll back to a known-good version. The analogy is about recording exactly what went in, which is the opposite of writing nothing down.

items.prompt: Which best describes the overall ML lifecycle?
choices: A straight line that ends permanently at 'deploy'
A single step where you train once and never touch it again
Only deploying, with no training or monitoring
A loop: collect data, train, deploy, monitor, then retrain and redeploy when needed
items.explanation: The lifecycle is a maintained loop that cycles through collecting data, training, deploying, monitoring, and then retraining and redeploying as the model slips. Treating it as a one-time line that ends at deploy ignores the ongoing monitoring and updates that real systems require.

items.prompt: How is 'retraining' a model different from simply redeploying the existing code?
choices: They are identical actions with two names
Retraining only changes the model's color
Retraining makes a new, updated model from new data; redeploying code alone just ships the same program again
Redeploying code always improves accuracy by itself
items.explanation: Retraining produces a fresh model by learning from new data, whereas redeploying unchanged code just puts the same program back without updating what the model learned. They are not the same, and shipping the same code again does nothing to refresh a model that has fallen behind the world.

items.prompt: What is a 'pipeline' in this MLOps context?
choices: A physical pipe that carries data between buildings
A set-up sequence of steps that runs on its own to automate parts of the loop
A single prediction returned to a user
A way to delete old models permanently
items.explanation: Here a pipeline is an automated sequence of steps that can run on its own, handling repetitive parts of the collect-train-deploy-monitor loop. It is not a literal pipe, a single prediction, or a deletion tool; its purpose is to automate the recurring work.

items.prompt: Two teammates run the SAME training code but on different snapshots of the data and get noticeably different models. What does this show?
choices: That code is the only thing worth versioning
That data does not affect models at all
That the data (and settings) also shape the model, so they must be versioned to reproduce a result
That one of the computers is broken
items.explanation: Identical code yielding different models from different data proves the data itself influences the outcome, so it must be versioned alongside the code and settings. This is the very reason code-only versioning is insufficient; nothing here points to broken hardware.

items.prompt: Why is it accurate to say an ML model is 'never really done'?
choices: Because models can never be deployed at all
Because training literally never finishes running
Because you must rewrite all the code every hour
Because the work is an ongoing loop: even after deploying, you keep monitoring and retraining as things change
items.explanation: After deployment the cycle continues: you monitor live behavior and retrain and redeploy as conditions shift, so maintenance never truly ends. It is not that training runs forever or that code is constantly rewritten; it is that the lifecycle is a continuing loop, not a finish line.

lessons.title: Monitoring, Data Drift, Retraining, and Responsible Production
lessons.content:
Now the most important ongoing risk in production, the one that catches beginners by surprise. A model that was accurate the day you deployed it can **silently get worse over time, even though nobody changed the code**. Why? Because the **world changes**. This lesson is about spotting that decay and responding to it, plus the honest cautions that come with running models for real.

**Hook.** Imagine a road map printed years ago. The map itself never changed, but new roads were built and old ones closed, so it slowly became wrong. You did not break the map; reality moved out from under it. To stay useful, the map must be **updated**.

**Build.** A deployed model learns patterns from its **training data**. Over time, the **live data** it sees can drift away from that training data: new slang appears, fraudsters invent new tricks, a sensor ages and reads differently, shopping habits shift. When the live data no longer matches what the model learned, its predictions get worse. This gradual mismatch is called **data drift** (or **concept drift**, when the very meaning of the right answer changes). Crucially, this is **not** a bug in the code; it is the model going stale as the world moves. This is a real way machine learning differs from ordinary software, which does not simply rot just because the world changed, the way a model's accuracy can.

The response has two parts. First, **monitoring**: continuously watch the model's **live performance** and its **inputs**, and set **alerts** so you are warned when quality slips or the incoming data looks different from before. Second, when it degrades, **retrain** the model on **fresh, recent data** so it relearns the new patterns, then **redeploy** the updated model. Together, monitoring plus retraining is what keeps a model accurate as conditions change, closing the loop you saw earlier.

A few more production-grade practices, in plain words. **A/B testing** means trying a new model on only a **slice of traffic** and comparing it against the old one before trusting it widely. **Rollback** means switching back to the previous model if the new one turns out worse. And some honest cautions: watch for **bias or unfairness** and unexpected failures once real users arrive; keep a **human in the loop** for high-stakes decisions (like medical or legal calls) rather than letting the model decide alone; and **log the predictions** so that when something goes wrong, you can trace what happened and why.

**Recap.** Building the model is only the start. In production a model can quietly decay as live data drifts from training data, that is data or concept drift, so you monitor live performance and inputs, set alerts, and retrain on fresh data when it slips. Add A/B testing, rollback, fairness checks, a human in the loop for high-stakes calls, and logging, and you keep an ML system reliable, reproducible, and accurate as the world changes.

items.prompt: What is 'data drift' (or 'concept drift') for a deployed model?
choices: A bug that corrupts the model's code file
When the live data gradually moves away from the training data, so the model's predictions get worse over time
When the model is copied to a faster computer
When the training data is deleted by accident
items.explanation: Data drift is the live inputs slowly diverging from what the model was trained on, which degrades its predictions even though the code is unchanged. It is not a corrupted file or a hardware move; it is the world shifting away from the patterns the model learned.

items.prompt: A fraud-detection model was accurate at launch but is slowly missing more fraud each month, and no one changed its code. What is the most likely explanation?
choices: The accuracy of a model can never change once deployed
The computer is running too fast
The model finished training, so it is now permanently perfect
Data drift: fraudsters changed their tricks, so live data no longer matches the training data
items.explanation: Fraudsters inventing new tactics is a textbook cause of drift: the live data shifts away from the training data and accuracy decays. A deployed model's accuracy absolutely can change as the world moves, so assuming it stays fixed or 'permanently perfect' is exactly the misconception to avoid.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best matches data drift?
choices: A road map that goes stale as roads are built and closed, so it must be updated
A calculator that always gives the same answer to the same sum
A book that never changes its words
A clock that keeps perfect time forever
items.explanation: A map slowly becoming wrong as the world's roads change captures drift: the model is unchanged but reality moved, so it must be refreshed. The other examples stay correct on their own and do not 'go stale' as conditions change, so they miss the point of drift.

items.prompt: Together, what do MONITORING and RETRAINING accomplish?
choices: They make the model train faster the first time
They remove the need to ever deploy the model
They guarantee the model will never need any updates
They detect when a model's quality slips and refresh it on new data, keeping it accurate as conditions change
items.explanation: Monitoring spots degradation by watching live performance and inputs, and retraining relearns the new patterns from fresh data, so together they keep a deployed model accurate over time. They do not speed up first-time training or eliminate deployment; in fact they exist precisely because models do need ongoing updates.

items.prompt: What is the point of 'monitoring' a model in production?
choices: To continuously watch live performance and inputs and alert you when quality slips or the data looks different
To make the model file smaller on disk
To pick the model's name
To delete the training data automatically
items.explanation: Monitoring means keeping an eye on how the model performs on live data and whether incoming inputs have shifted, raising alerts when something degrades. It is an early-warning system for drift and failures, not a tool for shrinking files or naming or deleting things.

items.prompt: How does an ML model's behavior over time differ from ordinary software?
choices: An ML model's accuracy can quietly decay as the world changes, while ordinary software does not simply rot just because the world changed
Ordinary software and ML models both rot automatically every week
ML models never change, but ordinary software always does
There is no difference at all
items.explanation: Ordinary software keeps doing exactly what it was written to do unless someone changes it, but an ML model can lose accuracy as live data drifts from its training data. That silent, world-driven decay is the key difference, which is why monitoring and retraining matter so much for ML.

items.prompt: What does 'A/B testing' a new model mean?
choices: Training the model with both letters A and B as labels
Deleting model A and keeping model B at random
Trying the new model on only a slice of traffic and comparing it against the current one before trusting it widely
Running the model twice on the same input
items.explanation: A/B testing sends part of the live traffic to the new model and the rest to the old one, then compares results so you adopt the new model only if it actually does better. It is a careful comparison on real traffic, not a labeling scheme or a coin-flip deletion.

items.prompt: A newly deployed model turns out to perform worse than the old one. What practice lets you respond safely?
choices: Rollback: switch back to the previous, known-good model
Ignore it, since new is always better
Immediately delete all versions of the model
Stop monitoring so you do not notice the problem
items.explanation: Rollback means reverting to the previous model that you know worked, which is why keeping versioned models matters. Assuming new is always better, deleting everything, or turning off monitoring would all make the situation worse rather than safely recovering from a bad release.

items.prompt: Which is a responsible production practice when a model makes high-stakes decisions (e.g. medical or legal)?
choices: Let the model decide entirely on its own to save time
Keep a human in the loop, watch for bias or unfairness, and log predictions so problems can be traced
Turn off all logging to keep things simple
Assume the model can never be biased or fail
items.explanation: For high-stakes calls you keep a human in the loop, watch for bias and unexpected failures, and log predictions so issues can be investigated. Letting the model act alone, disabling logging, or assuming it cannot fail removes exactly the safeguards that responsible deployment depends on.

items.prompt: Why is logging a model's predictions in production useful?
choices: It makes the model train without any data
It guarantees the model is always correct
It records what the model did so that when something goes wrong, you can trace and understand it
It permanently prevents data drift from ever happening
items.explanation: Logging predictions creates a record you can look back on to trace and diagnose problems when they appear. It does not train the model, make it infallible, or stop drift; it is about traceability so issues in production can be understood and fixed.

items.prompt: Pulling it together: which statement best sums up the role of MLOps?
choices: Once a model works in a notebook, the job is finished
MLOps is only about making predictions faster
MLOps means never deploying a model, to avoid all risk
Building the model is only the start; MLOps keeps it reliable, reproducible, and accurate as the world changes
items.explanation: MLOps takes over after the model first works, handling deployment, reproducibility, monitoring, and retraining so the system stays dependable as conditions shift. Thinking the job ends at a working notebook, or that MLOps is just about speed or avoiding deployment, misses its core purpose of maintaining models in the real world.
